chunk_summary: Emergency referral criteria for children with obesity focus on acute metabolic, cardiovascular, mental health, and orthopedic complications requiring immediate evaluation and stabilization.
learning_objectives: Identify emergency comorbidities in pediatric obesity requiring same-day referral.
Understand triage criteria for acute metabolic decompensation and hypertensive emergencies.
Recognize mental health crises and eating disorder instability in obese children.
Assess orthopedic and neurologic red flags for urgent imaging and specialist referral.
key_facts: Acute metabolic decompensation signs include polyuria, polydipsia, vomiting, Kussmaul breathing, and altered sensorium; refer immediately for suspected DKA.
Hypertensive emergency symptoms include severe headache, chest pain, dyspnea, neurologic deficits, or visual changes; confirmed very high BP requires urgent care.
Suicidal ideation with plan, self-harm, or severe depression mandates same-day psychiatric assessment.
Suspected SCFE (slipped capital femoral epiphysis) presents as acute hip/knee pain with limp or inability to bear weight; requires urgent imaging and non–weight-bearing instructions.
Suspected IIH (idiopathic intracranial hypertension) with headache and visual symptoms or papilledema necessitates urgent neuroimaging and ophthalmology referral.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: Explicit triage criteria prevent delayed care for time-sensitive complications and reduce medicolegal risk. (Source: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/36250645/)
document_key_topics: Emergency referral criteria for pediatric obesity complications
Mental health crises and eating disorder instability
Orthopedic and neurologic red flags in obese children
